title: #architecture: 1737762209
preview:
Jada Williams: Kicking off ADR-034 review re: unit econ cost attribution model. Main open items: allocation basis, idle capacity treatment, and making sure this lines up w/ Finance methodology (Rishi/Laura). Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/ARCH/ADR-034-unit-economics-cost-attribution-model

Cole Summers: thx. tl;dr options we captured:
1) allocate compute spend by output tokens
2) allocate by total tokens (in+out)
3) allocate by "effective GPU time" (gpu_ms) from runtime
4) hybrid fallback (gpu_ms where available, else perf coefficient * tokens)

Rishi Malhotra: From FP&A side: we need a definition that reconciles to fleet spend inputs + rate card. Allocation basis matters less than (a) stability + (b) ability to explain deltas. But we cannot have a model that swings purely b/c prompt lengths changed.

Laura Bennett: +1. For exec KPI, we want something we can defend quarter over quarter. If Product runs an experiment that changes average prompt length, the metric should reflect true spend per *delivered output* but we need to be explicit about what drives it.

Logan Wright: One issue w/ pure output token allocation: prefill costs get shoved into output denom, so long-context prompts make $/1M output tokens look "worse" even if decode path is stable. That might be OK but needs interpretation guidance.

Nadia Rahman: If we go gpu_ms: instrumentation quality is the risk. We have good coverage on decode gpu_time in runtime, but prefill vs decode split is still inconsistent across some models + streaming paths (we're fixing). Also multi-tenant batching complicates request-level gpu attribution.

Mei Lin: Warehouse POV: easiest to implement + backfill is token-based. gpu_ms will require joining runtime metrics and the metering events w/ consistent request_id. doable, but will extend v1 timeline unless we accept partial coverage + "confidence" flag.

Dylan Brooks: I don't want v1 to depend on perfect gpu_ms coverage. But I also don't love "output tokens" as allocation basis for compute. The whole point is showing drivers like caching/batching; token-only hides those.

Cole Summers: Proposed in ADR draft: allocate *cost pool* by gpu_ms where available; compute gpu_ms per request from runtime (post-batching) so it naturally captures batching gains. For cases w/ missing gpu_ms, fallback to `model_profile_cost_per_token * total_tokens` (profiles maintained by Quentin + ML).

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if we do hybrid, we need a clear data quality SLO: "% requests with gpu_ms present" + alert when it drops. Otherwise the metric will silently change its basis and Finance will (rightly) freak out.

Allison Grant: +1. also if fallback triggers, we should surface a banner in dashboard + tag the time buckets as degraded. We already do similar for trace sampling.

Rishi Malhotra: Hybrid is OK as long as:
- numerator ties to rate card + amortization rules
- we can quantify fallback usage (and ideally keep it low)
- changes in model_profile coefficients are versioned (so historical doesn’t get rewritten)

Jada Williams: On idle capacity: ADR currently says we include idle in blended metric (since exec cares about total economics) but we break out idle as separate component for drilldown. Any objections?

Laura Bennett: Include idle in blended, yes. BUT we need consistent treatment across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
- Dedicated: if customer pays a commit, do we treat their reserved-but-idle as "their" cost or platform idle?
- Hosted: idle is platform overhead, allocate across requests.

Cole Summers: In draft we suggested:
- Hosted fleet idle: allocate across hosted requests proportional to gpu_ms (or fallback basis)
- Dedicated idle: allocate to that customer tier (Dedicated) but not to a specific customer_id in v1 (privacy + contract nuance)
- Private: out of scope for shared allocation; private deployments have their own cost pool by contract (but dashboard still reports their $/1M output tokens based on their metered usage + their specific rate card)

Logan Wright: re: dedicated: if we allocate dedicated idle to dedicated usage, $/1M output tokens will spike when a customer is quiet. That is "true" blended unit economics, but will surprise people. We should show two modes? "blended" vs "utilized".

Rishi Malhotra: Finance needs blended (includes idle) for KPI. But I agree we should provide a "utilization-adjusted" view for ops so Eng isn't chasing noise when a big dedicated customer pauses traffic. Can that be a secondary metric?

Jada Williams: ADR mentions a secondary metric: "marginal compute $/1M output tokens" (excludes idle + shared overhead). Not in v1 UI, but we can calculate in warehouse and hide behind internal toggle.

Dylan Brooks: That works. But make sure no one screenshots marginal and sends to execs :)

Allison Grant: We can gate it behind `unit_econ.show_marginal=true` + watermark "internal / non-FP&A" in UI.

Nadia Rahman: Another nuance: allocation basis for overhead (control plane, networking, oncall) shouldn't use gpu_ms. That stuff scales more with requests/throughput. ADR propose: overhead pool allocated by request_count weighted by output_tokens? Thoughts?

Laura Bennett: Keep overhead allocation simple + stable. For v1, I'm ok with allocating overhead proportional to output tokens (or total tokens) as long as you document it. Don’t overfit.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. For reconciliation, we’ll likely have:
- Compute pool (GPU): allocated by gpu_ms (hybrid fallback)
- Non-GPU variable costs (eg egress): allocate by bytes_out or output_tokens proxy
- Fixed overhead: allocate by output_tokens
Just version the methodology.

Mei Lin: If we do multiple pools, warehouse schema should store columns: `gpu_cost_usd`, `overhead_cost_usd`, `egress_cost_usd`, `idle_cost_usd` + total. Then dashboard can stack.

Sean Gallagher: Please also include `coverage_gpu_ms_pct` per hour/model/region/tier in the aggregate table so we can alert and show confidence.

Jada Williams: Sounds like consensus:
- Primary KPI remains blended $/1M output tokens (includes idle + overhead)
- Compute allocation by gpu_ms where available, fallback to versioned model_profile coefficients
- Idle included, but surfaced as component; dedicated idle attributed to dedicated tier (not per customer)
- Add coverage/confidence signals + SLOs
I’ll update ADR-034 with this and tag for signoff.

Laura Bennett: 👍 Works for me, pending seeing the exact reconciliation steps written out.

Rishi Malhotra: same. Please include a note on month-close: rate card updates are versioned and applied prospectively for reporting stability.

Cole Summers: ack. I’ll add a section: `rate_card_version` join key + backfill policy (no silent rewrites). Also will call out timezone/day-boundary alignment to Finance calendar.

Logan Wright: Can we add one explicit "Known limitation" in ADR: $/1M output tokens is sensitive to prompt length b/c prefill cost is in numerator but denom is output. That’s expected; dashboard should show avg input tokens + prefill/decode ratio.

Jada Williams: good call. Adding that + linking to runtime signals doc.

Sean Gallagher: When ADR lands, loop SRE for alert routing + oncall ownership. We want the dependency graph documented.

Jada Williams: will do. Next step: I’ll post revised ADR and request approvals from @Laura @Rishi @Dylan by EOW.

title: #eng-platform: 1737750121
preview:
Logan Wright: Hey folks — need quick alignment on OTel span attribute names for route experiments before we lock PR-28489. We already tag `rw.route.id`, `rw.model.id`, etc. What should we use for experiment + variant?

Caleb Johnson: +1. Current draft in the PR uses `experiment_id` / `variant_id` (flat) which doesn’t match anything else we do. Also worried about collisions with generic “experiment” usage.

Allison Grant: We should follow our existing convention: lowercase, vendor prefix, dot-separated. OTel spec doesn’t require dots, but it’s what we’ve been doing for custom attrs (`rw.*`).

Jordan Lee: On the routing side we have fields named `experiment_id` and `variant_id` in the request context. Happy to map to whatever attribute keys we standardize.

Jada Williams: Also: please don’t add both “route_experiment” and “canary” attributes with different naming styles. Console is going to pivot on these.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: keep it consistent so it’s searchable in traces. Also, if we can avoid adding attributes on every child span that’d help. We only need them on the top-level request span + maybe inference span.

Chloe Martin: For the Console results API we’re joining on `{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id}`. If we pick names now, we should mirror those dimensions in telemetry.

Logan Wright: Proposed options (based on what’s in-flight):
1) `rw.experiment_id`, `rw.variant_id`
2) `rw.experiment.id`, `rw.experiment.variant_id`
3) `rw.route.experiment.id`, `rw.route.experiment.variant`

Caleb Johnson: I’d vote #2. Dot structure reads better, and we can later add `rw.experiment.allocation` or `rw.experiment.bucket` if needed without inventing new roots.

Allison Grant: Agree #2. Also keep `variant_id` vs `variant`? We’ve used `*.id` for IDs consistently.

Jordan Lee: Variant is an ID string anyway (like `control` / `v2-quant`), but being explicit doesn’t hurt.

Rafael Mendes: Whatever we pick, please document “omit when not in experiment” (don’t send empty string). Makes filters + cardinality less gross.

Logan Wright: Yep.

Jada Williams: One more: should it be `rw.experiment.variant.id` vs `rw.experiment.variant_id`? If we’re going dots, might as well be fully nested.

Allison Grant: I’d avoid going too deep for now. We don’t have any other `rw.foo.bar.baz` keys today besides a couple in auth. But I’m OK either way.

Caleb Johnson: Depth isn’t a big deal but let’s not bike-shed. I slightly prefer:
- `rw.experiment.id`
- `rw.experiment.variant_id`

Jordan Lee: Why not `rw.experiment.variant.id`?

Caleb Johnson: Because we’ll end up with 3 spellings across services. In practice folks type these in Grafana/Tempo search; shorter is better.

Rafael Mendes: +1 shorter. We can always add `rw.experiment.variant_name` later if we really need it.

Chloe Martin: Console is going to display “Variant” and store `variant_id`. I’d keep the attribute key matching the storage field name (`variant_id`).

Logan Wright: Ok. Another question: do we use underscores at all? Existing keys we have in spans today are mostly dot-separated but the leaf sometimes has underscore (`rw.cache.hit`, `rw.request.id` etc.).

Allison Grant: We should standardize on dot-separated segments, underscores allowed in the last segment if it improves readability, but ideally no. That said, `variant_id` is already pretty readable.

Jordan Lee: On assignment debug endpoint we’re returning `variant_id` too. Keeping `variant_id` makes copy/paste easier.

Caleb Johnson: Cool with underscores at the leaf if it avoids “variant.id” vs “variant_id” confusion.

Logan Wright: Proposed final:
- `rw.experiment.id` (string)
- `rw.experiment.variant_id` (string)

Also:
- only set on the root `router.request` span and the `inference.request` span
- do NOT emit when request isn’t in an experiment
- values are stable IDs (no long JSON blobs)

Rafael Mendes: Please also ensure we don’t tag raw paths; we already decided `rw.route.id` is normalized template not `/v1/chat/123` etc.

Jordan Lee: 👍 that’s already in router.

Caleb Johnson: Works. One more note for the PR: in OpenTelemetry, attribute keys must be strings; values should be short. Avoid adding `experiment_name` as it’s not stable.

Allison Grant: Can we add a small note to the internal conventions doc / ADR-048? Just so we don’t get `experimentId` in some other service later.

Logan Wright: Yep, I’ll add a follow-up in the PR + quick note in the telemetry schema doc.

Jada Williams: Any objection to prefixing with `rw.experiment.*` vs `redwood.*`? I prefer `rw.*` since everything else is already there.

Rafael Mendes: `rw.*` is fine.

Chloe Martin: +1.

Logan Wright: Decision: we’ll standardize OTel span attributes to:
- `rw.experiment.id`
- `rw.experiment.variant_id`

I’ll update PR-28489 + ping in #eng-sre when it’s ready for another look.

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: (and please include an example in the PR description)

Logan Wright: Will do. Example:
```
Attributes on router.request:
  rw.route.id = "chat.completions"
  rw.experiment.id = "exp_7f3c1b"
  rw.experiment.variant_id = "treatment_a"
```

Rafael Mendes: Great. Also add to the alert/runbook queries later, but this is enough to unblock.

Jordan Lee: thanks all — I’ll align the policy engine tags too so they match when we do span propagation there.

title: #eng-oncall: 1737506650
preview:
Ivy Chen: oncall q: how do we *validate audit log delivery* during a partial region outage? like us-east degraded but us-west ok. i want something more concrete than “check dashboards are green” before we tell Support/customers. what’s the current playbook?

Datadog: [ALERT] audit-ingest - delivery_latency_p95 breached (region=us-east-1, env=prod) | p95=92s threshold=60s

Sean Gallagher: good q. we don’t have a single “push button” validation yet, but there are a couple checks that work together.

Sean Gallagher: first: confirm whether we’re in “ingest backlog” vs “producer not emitting” vs “exporter blocked”. region outages can look like all 3 depending on which component is impacted.

Ivy Chen: yeah. specifically: if us-east is partially down, requests may still be served from us-west. how do we prove the audit events for those actions still make it to the sink + exports?

Rafael Mendes: runbook draft covers this, but it’s a bit buried. linking: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Audit+Log+Pipeline+Incident+Runbook (section “Regional impairment / failover validation”).

Kevin Osei: from exporter side: delivery is at-least-once. during region flaps you might see duplicates. validation should focus on: (1) events still arriving (2) no sustained drops (3) latency trending back down once region recovers.

Allison Grant: +1. also make sure you’re looking at the right graphs. audit pipeline is split:
- producer emit (service logs / counters)
- ingest accepted (gateway -> audit ingest)
- queue/buffer depth
- exporter success/error per target

Logan Wright: we’ve got the “expected vs observed” view in the coverage dashboard but it’s not perfect for real-time outage validation. still useful to sanity check that the sensitive event types are continuing to show up.

Ivy Chen: can someone give a concrete step list? like: “perform action X in region Y” and then “search for event_id Z”

Amara Diallo: for hosted/dedicated you can do a safe synthetic: hit the admin test endpoint? we don’t have a dedicated “audit test event” endpoint yet.

Sean Gallagher: we intentionally avoided a public “emit arbitrary audit event” endpoint. but we can do a low-risk sensitive action in a sandbox tenant.

Benji Okafor: easiest RBAC action in a non-prod tenant: create a temp role and delete it. that should emit:
- rbac.role.create
- rbac.role.delete
and it exercises actor + target + diff fields.

Nadia Rahman: if you’re validating *region failover*, make sure the request actually routes to the alternate region. in prod, you can force region via header in internal tooling, or do it from a host that prefers us-west. otherwise you may think you’re testing us-west but still hitting us-east.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes (also sensitive) there’s usually more guardrails. RBAC change in an internal test tenant is better.

Ivy Chen: ok, so:
1) do RBAC create/delete in sandbox tenant
2) confirm event shows up
but where do i look to confirm it “made it” end-to-end?

Kevin Osei: if you have access to the raw audit stream (internal), quickest is querying ingest store by correlation IDs.
- grab request_id / trace_id from the API response headers or service logs
- search audit-events index for request_id

Kevin Osei: if you’re validating SIEM export specifically, pick one configured internal Splunk HEC target (we have the staging one) and check the exporter “delivered_events_total{target=splunk_hec}” increments + sample payload includes event_id.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall, we usually do:
A) dashboards: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/audit-pipeline/audit-log-pipeline (latency, drop rate, queue depth by region)
B) spot check: search audit event store for the synthetic action by request_id
C) exporter check: exporter success rate + if customer is impacted, have Support ask for a sample of received events and verify event_id continuity.

Allison Grant: and for partial region outage, the key is comparing producer emits vs ingest accepts.
- if producer emits keep increasing but ingest accepts flatlines in us-east -> ingest path issue

Kevin Osei: from exporter side: delivery is at-least-once. during region flaps you might see duplicates. validation should focus on: (1) events still arriving (2) no sustained drops (3) latency trending back down once region recovers.

Allison Grant: +1. also make sure you’re looking at the right graphs. audit pipeline is split:
- producer emit (service logs / counters)
- ingest accepted (gateway -> audit ingest)

preview:
Chloe Martin: dropping the latest API contract sketch for unit econ dashboard queries. would love review esp on pagination + caching approach. doc here: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract (WIP)

Chloe Martin: TL;DR proposal: single POST endpoint for both timeseries + breakdowns:
`POST /internal/unit-economics/query`

request shape (draft):
```
{
  "metric": "blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens",
  "time": {"start": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z", "end": "2025-01-21T00:00:00Z", "grain": "hour"},
  "filters": {"model": ["llama3.1-70b"], "region": ["us-east-1"], "tier": ["self_serve"], "quant_profile": ["fp16"]},
  "group_by": ["model", "region"],
  "order_by": [{"field": "metric", "dir": "desc"}],
  "limit": 50,
  "cursor": null
}
```
response:
```
{
  "data": [...],
  "next_cursor": "...",
  "meta": {"partial": false, "cache": "hit"}
}
```

Chloe Martin: caching: API layer caches common queries (TTL ~60s) and we also rely on warehouse materialized views for speed. pagination: cursor-based for breakdown tables. timeseries returns full series for requested range (bounded by max window).

Felix Schneider: thx. quick q: are we sure we want one endpoint for both timeseries + topN breakdown? seems flexible but UI will end up with 2-3 fixed query shapes anyway. single endpoint makes caching keys tricky.

Jada Williams: +1 on caching keys. also single endpoint tends to become “anything goes” and then we have to support weird combos (group_by time + other dims + order_by metric??)

Nora Ahmed: from UI side we’ll call:
1) timeseries (single line, optionally compare prev period)
2) topN models
3) topN regions
4) topN tiers
and then drill to “detail table” w pagination

Nora Ahmed: if we can have predictable endpoints, it helps with client caching + type safety.

Chloe Martin: fair. i was trying to avoid endpoint explosion but agree we should constrain query shapes. what would you prefer?

Mei Lin: data side: timeseries and breakdown hit different views. timeseries is basically `mv_unit_econ_hourly` aggregated by dims + hour. breakdown is same but order/limit. i’d rather we expose explicit query types so we can map to the right MV and keep p95 down.

Logan Wright: also want guardrails on window sizes. finance likes QTD which is fine, but if someone asks 365d hourly * group_by(model,region,quant_profile,tier) it’s going to wreck the warehouse.

Allison Grant: +1. please make max ranges explicit and return a helpful error. we’ll get paged for “dashboard slow” otherwise.

Chloe Martin: ok. proposed split:
- `GET /internal/unit-economics/timeseries`
- `GET /internal/unit-economics/breakdown`
where breakdown supports group_by + topN + pagination

Chloe Martin: question: keep POST or move to GET w query params? GET makes caching/CDN easier but filters are arrays.

Felix Schneider: i’d do POST for breakdown (complex body) and GET for timeseries (simple). timeseries params are manageable: metric, start/end, grain, plus filters (repeatable query params)

Jada Williams: if we do GET + arrays, we’ll end up with `model=...&model=...` anyway. i’m fine w that for timeseries.

Nora Ahmed: +1. timeseries is the one we’ll refresh frequently and want to cache aggressively.

Sean Gallagher: re caching: please don’t “cache everything 60s” by default. we need to be explicit about freshness expectations. finance view vs live view came up in SRE thread.

Allison Grant: yes. recommend two modes:
- `view=live` (low TTL, allows partial latest hour)
- `view=finance` (hourly finalized, longer TTL, no partial)
then our alerting can treat finance view as “source of truth”.

Chloe Martin: i saw that thread. we can add `view` param default `live` for eng users, and Finance can pin `finance`.

Logan Wright: “finance” should probably align to the hourly job watermark and only expose hours <= watermark. otherwise we’ll keep arguing about missing last 20 mins.

Mei Lin: +1. we can materialize `unit_econ_hourly_finance` which lags by 1-2 hours but is stable.

Chloe Martin: ok, so:
`GET /internal/unit-economics/timeseries?metric=...&start=...&end=...&grain=hour&view=finance&model=...&region=...&tier=...&quant_profile=...`

Chloe Martin: breakdown:
`POST /internal/unit-economics/breakdown`
body:
```
{
  "metric": "blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens",
  "time": {"start": "...", "end": "...", "grain": "day"},
  "view": "finance",
  "filters": {"tier": ["self_serve", "dedicated"], "region": ["us-east-1"]},
  "group_by": ["model"],
  "sort": {"field": "metric", "dir": "desc"},
  "limit": 25,
  "cursor": null
}
```

Felix Schneider: pagination: please don’t do offset/limit. cursor should be stable based on sort key. but if sort key can tie (same metric), you need tie-breaker.

Jada Williams: +1. cursor should encode (sort_value, dimension_key) at least.

Chloe Martin: yep. proposal: order is (metric_value DESC, group_key ASC). cursor encodes last row’s metric_value + group_key. if group_by has multiple dims, group_key is a canonical string like `model=...|region=...|tier=...`.

Nora Ahmed: question: UI wants “Top 10 + Others” bar sometimes. do we get total count?

Chloe Martin: count is expensive. could return `meta.total_groups_estimate`?? or we can request `include_totals=true` for a slower path.

Mei Lin: pls don’t add totals v1 unless needed. counting distinct groups across huge windows is not cheap.

Felix Schneider: agree. for “Others”, UI can sum the returned topN and label remainder as “not shown”.

Nora Ahmed: works.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: authz note: these endpoints must be behind internal feature flag + RBAC. contract should include 403 vs 404 behavior (we typically return 404 for disabled feature, 403 for not authorized).

Chloe Martin: good call. i’ll follow the console pattern: 
- feature flag off => 404
- flag on but user not in allowlist => 403

Dylan Brooks: +1 and we should log access for auditing (at least user id + query type + dims requested). not row-level sensitive but still econ data.

Allison Grant: also rate limit. once finance starts pinning dashboards on TVs we’ll see bursts. suggest server-side caching per query key + a per-user/IP rate limit.

Sean Gallagher: +1. and please add circuit breaker behavior if warehouse is slow (serve stale cache for finance view is OK). for live view we can fail open with “data temporarily unavailable”.

Chloe Martin: caching policy suggestion:
- timeseries live: TTL 60s, stale-while-revalidate 30s
- timeseries finance: TTL 10m, stale-if-error 60m
- breakdown live: TTL 2m
- breakdown finance: TTL 15m
and hard max time range per grain (e.g. hour <= 31d, day <= 365d)

Felix Schneider: sounds reasonable. would also be nice if response includes `meta.watermark` (latest finalized hour) so UI can show “data through 10:00Z”.

Chloe Martin: yes adding `meta.watermark_ts`.

Logan Wright: please also surface `meta.partial=true` only for live view when latest hour incomplete, and never for finance.

Chloe Martin: ack.

Jada Williams: contract question: dimension names. we should align with token metering dimension standard (region_normalized, customer_tier, model_id, model_version, quant_profile). UI labels can map separately.

Mei Lin: strongly +1. also region should be normalized already. no raw cloud region strings.

Chloe Martin: ok: API exposes canonical dimension keys (e.g. `region`, `tier`, `model`, `model_version`, `quant_profile`). internally we map to warehouse columns `region_norm`, etc. (to avoid leaking internal column names). but values must be canonical, not aliases.

Nora Ahmed: will we allow grouping by model_version in v1? product asked for it but it increases cardinality.

Logan Wright: allow it but only when also filtering to a specific model family (or limit time range). otherwise it’s 500 rows per model.

Allison Grant: could enforce server-side rules: if group_by contains model_version then require filter.model present OR limit<=50 + short range.

Chloe Martin: yep, we can validate and return 400 with message.

Felix Schneider: for cursor stability, note float ordering issues. metric is a decimal. better to order by a rounded value or order by (numerator_cost, denominator_tokens)??

Mei Lin: warehouse stores cost as DECIMAL and tokens as BIGINT. metric computed as `cost / tokens * 1e6`. ordering by computed metric is fine if we order by (cost, tokens, group_key) maybe?

Jada Williams: easiest: in the view, compute `metric_value` as DECIMAL(38, 12) and order by that. cursor stores stringified decimal.

Chloe Martin: ok.

Sean Gallagher: operational note: include query fingerprint in logs + metrics (but careful about cardinality). ex: hash(metric + view + group_by + grain + filter_keys only). don’t put raw model IDs in labels.

Allison Grant: +1, we’ve been burned by metric label cardinality.

Chloe Martin: will do.

Dylan Brooks: seems directionally good. action items:
1) Chloe update doc w split endpoints + view param + watermark + validation rules
2) Felix/Jada sanity-check cursor encoding + tie-breaker
3) Allison/Sean review caching TTL + rate limits for SRE signoff

Chloe Martin: 👍 will post updated contract tomorrow.

Felix Schneider: thx. also pls add examples for:
- compare period (prev range)
- breakdown by (region,tier)
- error response for invalid range

Chloe Martin: noted.

[Next day]

Chloe Martin: updated doc w endpoints + examples: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract#v2
highlights:
- GET /timeseries (supports `compare=previous_period`)
- POST /breakdown (cursor pagination)
- `view=live|finance`, response includes `meta.watermark_ts`, `meta.partial`
- validation: max window + restricted group_by combos

Allison Grant: reviewed. caching policy looks ok. one tweak: for finance view, TTL 15m is fine but set `stale-if-error=24h` so we don’t flap exec review if warehouse hiccups.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also add explicit 503 vs 504 semantics? if warehouse timeout, return 503 with `meta.stale_served=true` when we serve stale cache.

Chloe Martin: cool. added `meta.stale_served` + `meta.cache_age_seconds`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section looks good. please ensure endpoints are under `/internal/...` and also gated by `X-Redwood-Internal: true` header like other internal console APIs.

Chloe Martin: yep.

Jada Williams: cursor encoding: please base64(json) not base64(“string|string”). easier to evolve. but we can still treat it as opaque.

Chloe Martin: changed to base64(JSON) with fields: `last_metric_value`, `last_group_key`. server treats unknown fields as ignore.

Mei Lin: 👍 from data side. when you implement, please default breakdown grain to `day` (hourly breakdown tables get big fast).

Nora Ahmed: works. UI will use day unless user toggles hour.

Dylan Brooks: looks ready for ENG-8427. ship it.

Mei Lin: warehouse stores cost as DECIMAL and tokens as BIGINT. metric computed as `cost / tokens * 1e6`. ordering by computed metric is fine if we order by (cost, tokens, group_key) maybe?

Jada Williams: easiest: in the view, compute `metric_value` as DECIMAL(38, 12) and order by that. cursor stores stringified decimal.

Chloe Martin: ok.

Sean Gallagher: operational note: include query fingerprint in logs + metrics (but careful about cardinality). ex: hash(metric + view + group_by + grain + filter_keys only). don’t put raw model IDs in labels.

Allison Grant: +1, we’ve been burned by metric label cardinality.

Chloe Martin: will do.

Dylan Brooks: seems directionally good. action items:
1) Chloe update doc w split endpoints + view param + watermark + validation rules
2) Felix/Jada sanity-check cursor encoding + tie-breaker
3) Allison/Sean review caching TTL + rate limits for SRE signoff

Chloe Martin: 👍 will post updated contract tomorrow.

Felix Schneider: thx. also pls add examples for:
- compare period (prev range)
- breakdown by (region,tier)
- error response for invalid range

Chloe Martin: noted.

[Next day]

Chloe Martin: updated doc w endpoints + examples: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract#v2
highlights:
- GET /timeseries (supports `compare=previous_period`)
- POST /breakdown (cursor pagination)
- `view=live|finance`, response includes `meta.watermark_ts`, `meta.partial`
- validation: max window + restricted group_by combos

Allison Grant: reviewed. caching policy looks ok. one tweak: for finance view, TTL 15m is fine but set `stale-if-error=24h` so we don’t flap exec review if warehouse hiccups.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also add explicit 503 vs 504 semantics? if warehouse timeout, return 503 with `meta.stale_served=true` when we serve stale cache.

Chloe Martin: cool. added `meta.stale_served` + `meta.cache_age_seconds`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section looks good. please ensure endpoints are under `/internal/...` and also gated by `X-Redwood-Internal: true` header like other internal console APIs.

Chloe Martin: yep.

Jada Williams: cursor encoding: please base64(json) not base64(“string|string”). easier to evolve. but we can still treat it as opaque.

Chloe Martin: changed to base64(JSON) with fields: `last_metric_value`, `last_group_key`. server treats unknown fields as ignore.

Mei Lin: 👍 from data side. when you implement, please default breakdown grain to `day` (hourly breakdown tables get big fast).

Nora Ahmed: works. UI will use day unless user toggles hour.

title: #eng-platform: 1737381192
preview:
Logan Wright: Kicking off thread on the usage-insights aggregated dataset design. We need to lock: time bucket granularity, partitioning, late events handling, and who owns any backfill. Goal is route+model breakdowns that reconcile w/ billing.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Billing side constraint: invoices are computed in daily windows (UTC) but customers view in local time in console. We can *display* local-time but the dataset should be anchored in UTC or we’ll never reconcile.

Chloe Martin: For console queries we need interactive latency. If we bucket hourly we get nicer “what changed” and charts, but query cost might be higher. If we bucket daily it’s easier but UX wants hourly drilldowns.

Thomas Baird: On partitioning: please don’t do hour partitions at the top-level unless we’re 100% sure. Warehouse (Snowflake) likes partitions by day and clustering on dimensions. Hour partitions explode file counts and late events get painful.

Mei Lin: Current metering events volume is ~2–4B/day across all workspaces. For beta accounts subset it’s smaller, but the *dataset* should scale. My default: partition by `event_date` (UTC day), cluster by `workspace_id` + maybe `project_id`.

Dylan Brooks: UX ask: default time ranges are 24h / 7d / 30d. For 24h view, hourly granularity is important; table can show hour bars + spikes. For 7d/30d, daily is fine.

Logan Wright: Proposal: store hourly buckets but partition by day. I.e. `bucket_start_ts` is hour-aligned, but physical partition = `bucket_date`. That gives hourly detail without hour partitions.

Thomas Baird: That’s fine. Just be explicit: `bucket_start_ts` is UTC hour start. Partition column is `bucket_date` (YYYY-MM-DD). Cluster keys: `workspace_id`, `project_id`, `route_canonical`, `model_family`, `deployment_mode`, `region`.

Caleb Johnson: Metric quality note: late events are real. We see 0.5–2% of metering events arriving >1h late in one region during peak. If the job finalizes too quickly we’ll show “missing cost” and then it pops in later.

Allison Grant: Also we need to keep a freshness indicator. If we’re allowing late-event updates for N hours, console should show “data complete through <timestamp>” rather than implying it’s final.

Cole Summers: Reconciliation: invoice totals are based on billing hooks which run w/ their own lag. We should define tolerance + window. If usage insights keeps mutating last 72h, people will compare to an invoice generated earlier and file tickets.

Nadia Rahman: Yeah. For correctness, I’d like a clear policy:
- D-0 to D-2: mutable, expect updates (late events, retries attribution fixes)
- D-3 and older: treated as sealed except for backfill
And UI should label it.

Chloe Martin: From API standpoint, if the dataset mutates we need cache invalidation. If we do 24h lookback and it’s still changing, we should set caching to like 60s max.

Thomas Baird: Late events handling design: simplest is “recompute last N days” each run. But N=7 days will be expensive. N=2 might be too short.

Mei Lin: What if we do incremental + a small rolling correction window? E.g. job runs hourly:
- Upsert current hour continuously
- Recompute full day for today (D0) and yesterday (D1)
- Optionally D2 once per day
That matches Nadia’s policy.

Logan Wright: I like: hourly run recompute D0 + D1; daily run recompute D2. Past that only via backfill job.

Caleb Johnson: Need to confirm our watermarking. In ingestion we have `ingest_ts` and `event_ts`. Late-ness should be based on `ingest_ts - event_ts`. But if clock skew exists, we should clamp.

Jada Williams: Route normalization complicates late events too: if the canonicalization rules change, old rows need to be rewritten or you get splits (e.g. /v1/chat/completions vs /v1/chat/completions/{deployment}). That’s a schema/backfill issue.

Dylan Brooks: +1. We can’t ship a dataset that breaks when route normalization updates. For beta, can we freeze normalization rules?

Nadia Rahman: Freeze rules short-term, but we’ll still discover missing route tags (streaming endpoints) and we’ll need to backfill to avoid “unknown route” bucket.

Thomas Baird: Which brings up ownership: who owns backfill? Telemetry platform can run backfills but we need clear requester/approver + budget. Historically, backfills land on my team and we get paged for warehouse costs.

Logan Wright: My take:
- Telemetry & Analytics (my team) owns the dataset pipeline and any *platform-required* backfills (schema changes, normalization changes)
- Product/Console team owns “nice-to-have” reprocessing requests
But for this project, backfill is required to make totals match billing and route attribution correct.

Chloe Martin: Console backend team can’t run backfill jobs. We can define requirements + validate, but execution should be Telemetry.

Cole Summers: From billing: if we ship beta and totals drift, we’ll get support escalations. So I’d categorize backfill to fix missing attribution fields as “platform-required”.

Allison Grant: Agree. We should document a runbook: when drift > X% for a workspace, trigger backfill request. Include who approves and the max lookback.

Mei Lin: Re: partitions: if we anticipate backfills, day partitions are definitely better. Backfill will just overwrite day partitions (or do MERGE) for a specific date range.

Thomas Baird: Yup. And please keep the dimension cardinality sane. Route is the scariest dimension. If route_canonical isn’t stable we’ll pay twice.

Logan Wright: Draft schema (stringified here):
```sql
CREATE TABLE usage_insights_route_model_hourly (
  bucket_start_ts TIMESTAMP_NTZ, -- hour start UTC
  bucket_date DATE,              -- derived from bucket_start_ts
  workspace_id STRING,
  project_id STRING,
  environment STRING,            -- prod/staging/dev if present
  deployment_mode STRING,        -- hosted/dedicated/private
  region STRING,
  route_canonical STRING,
  model_family STRING,
  model_version STRING,
  model_alias STRING,
  tokens_in NUMBER,
  tokens_out NUMBER,
  cache_hit_tokens NUMBER,
  cache_miss_tokens NUMBER,
  retries_count NUMBER,
  fallbacks_count NUMBER,
  requests_count NUMBER,
  streaming_requests_count NUMBER,
  cost_usd NUMBER(38, 10),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP_NTZ
)
PARTITION BY (bucket_date);
```

Nadia Rahman: Looks good. `model_alias` might cause duplicate rollups though. For reporting we should prefer stable fields: family+version; alias only as display. (We can store alias but it shouldn’t be in the group-by key unless we really want it.)

Thomas Baird: +1. Avoid grouping by alias. Keep it as an attribute selected via MAX() or “most common alias” per bucket, or store in a separate mapping table.

Chloe Martin: For console filters users *do* select a model by the name they know. If alias changes, filter results will look weird.

Logan Wright: Let’s separate: dataset groups by `model_id` (stable), and we maintain mapping table for display name / aliases. Then console can filter by model_id but show alias.

Dylan Brooks: As long as API supports filtering by “model” with either alias or id. We can resolve alias -> id in query service.

Caleb Johnson: Late events: what’s the SLA for “complete”? If we’re showing hourly, I’d prefer we mark last 2 hours as “still updating”.

Allison Grant: UI/UX can show a banner like “Data may be delayed by up to 2 hours” + a precise “complete through” watermark. That should tie to the dataset watermark.

Cole Summers: For reconciliation vs billing: are we aligning cost_usd to the same rounding rules? Billing uses 1e-6 dollars internally then rounds on invoice. If insights rounds earlier, it will drift.

Nadia Rahman: We should store high precision (like NUMBER(38,10) as Logan wrote) and only round at display/export layer.

Mei Lin: Backfill question: can we cap historical backfill to retention? Telemetry raw metering retention is 30 days in the hot store and 90 days in archive (slower). If we promise 6 months of insights later, we need a plan.

Thomas Baird: For beta we should be explicit: insights available up to 30 days (or 90 if we can tolerate archive compute). Backfill beyond that will be painful.

Logan Wright: Action items:
1) I’ll write up a short design note: partition by day, store hourly buckets, mutable window D0-D2.
2) Nadia + Cole define reconciliation window/tolerance policy.
3) Thomas + Mei propose backfill ownership + approval process + max lookback.
4) Allison + Caleb provide freshness/late-event metrics we should surface.

Dylan Brooks: 👍 Please tag me on the doc. Console team needs a firm “complete through” definition to implement banners.

Thomas Baird: One more thing: do we want `bucket_start_ts` as TIMESTAMP_NTZ or TIMESTAMP_TZ? If we anchor on UTC, NTZ is fine and cheaper. But we must be strict on semantics.

Nadia Rahman: NTZ is fine if we explicitly say UTC. The API can always emit ISO8601 with Z.

Mei Lin: For late-event recompute, MERGE strategy: overwrite partitions for D0/D1 each run. That’s easiest and avoids per-row updates. Snowflake will handle it.

Logan Wright: ok. Will proceed with “overwrite day partition” for D0/D1 and daily for D2. Backfill job will also be “overwrite date range”.

Allison Grant: Please ensure we emit an audit metric when a partition is overwritten via backfill vs normal run. Helps explain why charts change.

Caleb Johnson: +1, and add a label in the dataset metadata: `recompute_reason` maybe not in the table but in job logs.

Jada Williams: Also: any schema change that adds a new attribution field (e.g. cache hit tokens) should trigger at least D0-D2 backfill, otherwise we’ll show zeros and confuse users.

Logan Wright: Noted. We’ll treat new columns as “null until computed”; query layer should handle nulls and label “not available for this time range” if needed.

Thomas Baird: Cool. Once doc is up, I’ll sign off on partition/clustering and the recompute window.

Logan Wright: thanks all. I’ll post the doc link here later today.

title: #eng-platform: 1737332112
preview:
Nadia Rahman: Kicking off the metric definition alignment thread for the Unit Econ dashboard. We need a crisp definition for “blended $/1M output tokens” that Finance will sign off on and Eng can implement consistently. Biggest open items: (1) idle capacity, (2) shared overhead (control plane / observability / support), (3) dedicated/private pools.

Logan Wright: +1. We keep saying “blended” but people interpret it as marginal. We should be explicit: numerator is what cost buckets, denominator is what tokens (output only vs total), and what time window / allocation basis.

Rishi Malhotra: From FP&A perspective, blended should reconcile to infra spend. That means idle is *in* (otherwise you’ll never tie out). The question is *where* we allocate it and whether we show a separate “active-only” view.

Cole Summers: Current draft in the PRD says primary metric = total cost / total output tokens. But “total cost” is still fuzzy. If we include idle GPU hours, do we allocate them across all tokens in that region+pool? Or keep idle as its own line item and the metric includes it by default?

Laura Bennett: Finance needs a number that: (a) doesn’t move around because engineering changed a label, (b) aligns with our QTD reporting, (c) can be explained in an exec deck. I’m okay with a “blended (fully loaded)” as primary, but we must avoid mixing pools.

Mei Lin: Implementation detail: aggregation job is hourly by (model_id, region, tier, quant_profile, streaming_flag). If idle is allocated, we need a “capacity_pool” dimension (hosted shared vs dedicated cluster X vs private) or we’ll smear costs across tiers.

Nadia Rahman: On denominator: are we comfortable using *output tokens* only? Some folks asked for total tokens (input+output) since prefill dominates certain workloads.

Rishi Malhotra: For the exec KPI, output tokens is fine (it’s also how we talk about revenue for generation). But we should also store input tokens and total tokens for diagnostics. The KPI name explicitly says output.

Logan Wright: Agree. Dashboard primary = $/1M output tokens. Drilldowns can show prefill/decode ratio and input tokens so we can explain shifts.

Jada Williams: For shared overhead: do we mean “shared infra overhead” like logging, tracing, control plane compute, storage, networking? If we try to build a perfect allocation model now we’ll get stuck.

Laura Bennett: We already have an overhead allocation in our monthly close (platform overhead bucket allocated by usage). If you want alignment, use that same factor. We can version it quarterly and keep it stable.

Cole Summers: So something like: numerator = GPU compute (active + idle) + network/egress + storage + overhead uplift factor, all at hourly granularity? Then allocate down to dimensions based on token-weighted usage?

Mei Lin: Token-weighted allocation works if cost is mostly variable with tokens. But idle is independent of tokens, so you’re distributing a fixed cost across the same denominator (tokens). That’s ok for blended, but then it’ll spike when volume drops.

Sean Gallagher: (SRE hat) +1 that volume drops will look like cost regressions. That’s fine if we’re explicit. But we should include a “confidence/coverage” indicator + maybe a note when total tokens are below some threshold (noisy).

Caleb Johnson: On data quality: whatever definition we pick, we need checks for missing output tokens. If streaming is undercounted, blended cost will look inflated because cost is there but tokens are missing.

Nadia Rahman: Back to idle capacity. Two cases:
1) Hosted shared pool: we keep headroom for latency SLOs.
2) Dedicated: customers pay commit; capacity might be idle but it’s “reserved for them.”
Do we treat dedicated idle as cost of that tier even if they don’t send traffic?

Rishi Malhotra: For Dedicated, yes. The cost exists and is attributable to that customer tier. Otherwise Dedicated would look artificially cheap when utilization is low.

Laura Bennett: Careful: dedicated pools are sometimes partially used for overflow during incidents (with approvals). We need rules for cross-charging if hosted borrows dedicated.

Sean Gallagher: We do borrow, but it’s rare + logged. If we can’t do cross-charging in v1, at least exclude those hours from the dedicated pool costs or tag them as “shared usage.”

Cole Summers: We can get a signal from the scheduler logs for “borrowed capacity” but it’s not in the cost pipeline today. Might be a v1.1 item.

Logan Wright: Suggest v1: cost attribution boundaries = pool-level. If hosted uses dedicated temporarily, it’s still counted in dedicated pool spend (since that’s how billing hits). Add an “exceptions/limitations” note. Otherwise we’re inventing allocation we can’t validate.

Laura Bennett: I’m okay with that for v1 if we document it clearly.

Nadia Rahman: What about idle allocation basis within a pool? Options:
A) allocate idle $ across all dims proportionally to active GPU time
B) allocate idle $ across dims proportionally to output tokens
C) keep idle as separate and blended metric uses (active+idle)/tokens anyway
A and B are basically equivalent once you pick the denominator, but it affects drilldowns.

Mei Lin: If we want drilldowns by model/version/quant profile, token-weighted makes the most sense because that’s what the denominator is. Otherwise a model with long sequences could get misallocated if it uses more GPU per token.

Logan Wright: But that’s the point — GPU per token differences are what we’re trying to measure. If you allocate idle by tokens, you’re penalizing high volume models more even if they’re efficient.

Rishi Malhotra: Finance view: allocation should reflect what “consumes” the capacity. In our reporting, we allocate shared costs by usage volume (tokens/requests). It’s not perfect but consistent.

Jada Williams: Alternative: allocate idle by “reserved target capacity” per model route? But we don’t have stable mapping of reserved headroom per model today.

Sean Gallagher: Also headroom is a policy knob and changes with incident posture. We don’t want the KPI changing because SRE bumped headroom during a bad week.

Caleb Johnson: We can add a second metric: “active $/1M output tokens” where numerator excludes idle (only billable GPU-seconds while executing). That helps engineering diagnose efficiency while keeping blended for finance.

Laura Bennett: I like having both, but the dashboard needs one primary headline. “Blended (fully loaded)” as primary, “active-only” as diagnostic secondary.

Logan Wright: If we do both, we must ensure they share denominator and differ only in idle inclusion. Otherwise it becomes a mess.

Nadia Rahman: Ok so proposal:
- Primary KPI: blended_fully_loaded_cost_per_1m_output_tokens = (active_cost + idle_cost + overhead_alloc + ancillary_costs) / output_tokens * 1e6
- Secondary: active_cost_per_1m_output_tokens = (active_cost + overhead_alloc? TBD) / output_tokens * 1e6
Question: does “overhead_alloc” go into active-only?

Rishi Malhotra: For active-only, I’d exclude overhead and keep it purely variable compute. But then it won’t match any finance number. That’s fine if it’s clearly labeled “runtime efficiency.”

Laura Bennett: Agree. Executive reporting uses fully loaded. Engineering diagnostics can use a cleaner marginal-ish metric.

Cole Summers: Shared overhead definition: Finance overhead is monthly, but pipeline is hourly. We can apply a monthly overhead factor (e.g., +X%) to all pool costs during that month. That gives hourly values without inventing new distributions.

Mei Lin: That’s easy to implement as a join on (month, cost_center/pool) with a factor.

Logan Wright: Need to be careful with retroactive changes (Finance adjusts overhead after close). If overhead factor changes, historical blended KPI changes. Is that acceptable?

Rishi Malhotra: We can version the factor and only revise closed periods with a backfill + changelog. For “live” dashboard view, we can use a provisional factor and mark it as preliminary until close.

Sean Gallagher: That ties into SLO/alerts too: if we backfill closed periods, dashboard charts will change; users will think it’s a regression. We should show a “data status: preliminary/final” badge.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also: do we include non-GPU infra (CPU gateway, redis cache) as overhead or separate line? It’s material for embeddings.

Laura Bennett: If it’s in infra spend and not tied to a specific token meter, it’s overhead. If you can attribute directly (e.g., per-request cache cluster) you can break it out later. Keep it simple.

Jada Williams: Summarizing what I’m hearing (tell me if wrong):
1) Blended = includes idle + shared overhead, reconciles to spend.
2) Active-only = excludes idle + overhead, used for eng diagnostics.
3) Allocation across dimensions should follow the same basis as denominator (output tokens), at least for v1.

Logan Wright: I’m not fully sold on token-weighted allocation for idle, but I can live with it if we keep pool boundaries strict and make sure the ADR states the tradeoff.

Cole Summers: I’ll update ADR-034 draft with:
- Idle included in blended, excluded in active-only
- Overhead included in blended, excluded in active-only
- Pool-level attribution as first-class (hosted shared / dedicated / private)
- Dimension allocation basis = output tokens within pool

Laura Bennett: Please add explicit inclusion/exclusion list (what costs are in numerator) and a reconciliation note (which GL lines / provider bills).

Rishi Malhotra: Action item from my side: write a 1-pager methodology note with examples + how we handle revisions (prelim vs final). I’ll drop in finance channel once drafted.

Caleb Johnson: From data quality: I’ll add a check that compares total output tokens (metering) vs expected tokens derived from request logs for streaming paths, since that’s where we’ve seen undercount. If mismatch > threshold, we mark blended metric as “low confidence.”

Sean Gallagher: Please route “low confidence” alerts to #eng-sre and #eng-platform; we’ll need a runbook link.

Mei Lin: I’ll adjust the warehouse views to emit both metrics (blended_fully_loaded, active_only) plus components columns (active_cost_usd, idle_cost_usd, overhead_usd) so UI can show breakdown.

Nadia Rahman: Great. Let’s keep debate open on allocation basis for idle (token-weighted vs gpu-time-weighted). If anyone has strong objections, reply here by EOW so we don’t ship a definition people hate.

Dylan Brooks: :eyes: catching up late. Strong preference: one “finance-aligned blended” that matches spend, plus an “engineering efficiency” metric. As long as we label them clearly and don’t let the blended one be interpreted as marginal, I’m good.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also please add tooltips in UI that explicitly say “blended includes idle capacity and overhead; expect this to rise when volume drops.”

Logan Wright: Noted. I’ll make sure PRD language is unambiguous (blended != marginal).

Nadia Rahman: Kicking off the metric definition alignment thread for the Unit Econ dashboard. We need a crisp definition for “blended $/1M output tokens” that Finance will sign off on and Eng can implement consistently. Biggest open items: (1) idle capacity, (2) shared overhead (control plane / observability / support), (3) dedicated/private pools.

Logan Wright: +1. We keep saying “blended” but people interpret it as marginal. We should be explicit: numerator is what cost buckets, denominator is what tokens (output only vs total), and what time window / allocation basis.

Rishi Malhotra: From FP&A perspective, blended should reconcile to infra spend. That means idle is *in* (otherwise you’ll never tie out). The question is *where* we allocate it and whether we show a separate “active-only” view.

Cole Summers: Current draft in the PRD says primary metric = total cost / total output tokens. But “total cost” is still fuzzy. If we include idle GPU hours, do we allocate them across all tokens in that region+pool? Or keep idle as its own line item and the metric includes it by default?

Laura Bennett: Finance needs a number that: (a) doesn’t move around because engineering changed a label, (b) aligns with our QTD reporting, (c) can be explained in an exec deck. I’m okay with a “blended (fully loaded)” as primary, but we must avoid mixing pools.

Mei Lin: Implementation detail: aggregation job is hourly by (model_id, region, tier, quant_profile, streaming_flag). If idle is allocated, we need a “capacity_pool” dimension (hosted shared vs dedicated cluster X vs private) or we’ll smear costs across tiers.

Nadia Rahman: On denominator: are we comfortable using *output tokens* only? Some folks asked for total tokens (input+output) since prefill dominates certain workloads.

Rishi Malhotra: For the exec KPI, output tokens is fine (it’s also how we talk about revenue for generation). But we should also store input tokens and total tokens for diagnostics. The KPI name explicitly says output.

Logan Wright: Agree. Dashboard primary = $/1M output tokens. Drilldowns can show prefill/decode ratio and input tokens so we can explain shifts.

Jada Williams: For shared overhead: do we mean “shared infra overhead” like logging, tracing, control plane compute, storage, networking? If we try to build a perfect allocation model now we’ll get stuck.

Laura Bennett: We already have an overhead allocation in our monthly close (platform overhead bucket allocated by usage). If you want alignment, use that same factor. We can version it quarterly and keep it stable.

Cole Summers: So something like: numerator = GPU compute (active + idle) + network/egress + storage + overhead uplift factor, all at hourly granularity? Then allocate down to dimensions based on token-weighted usage?

Mei Lin: Token-weighted allocation works if cost is mostly variable with tokens. But idle is independent of tokens, so you’re distributing a fixed cost across the same denominator (tokens). That’s ok for blended, but then it’ll spike when volume drops.

Sean Gallagher: (SRE hat) +1 that volume drops will look like cost regressions. That’s fine if we’re explicit. But we should include a “confidence/coverage” indicator + maybe a note when total tokens are below some threshold (noisy).

Caleb Johnson: On data quality: whatever definition we pick, we need checks for missing output tokens. If streaming is undercounted, blended cost will look inflated because cost is there but tokens are missing.

Nadia Rahman: Back to idle capacity. Two cases:
1) Hosted shared pool: we keep headroom for latency SLOs.
2) Dedicated: customers pay commit; capacity might be idle but it’s “reserved for them.”
Do we treat dedicated idle as cost of that tier even if they don’t send traffic?

Rishi Malhotra: For Dedicated, yes. The cost exists and is attributable to that customer tier. Otherwise Dedicated would look artificially cheap when utilization is low.

Laura Bennett: Careful: dedicated pools are sometimes partially used for overflow during incidents (with approvals). We need rules for cross-charging if hosted borrows dedicated.

Sean Gallagher: We do borrow, but it’s rare + logged. If we can’t do cross-charging in v1, at least exclude those hours from the dedicated pool costs or tag them as “shared usage.”

Cole Summers: We can get a signal from the scheduler logs for “borrowed capacity” but it’s not in the cost pipeline today. Might be a v1.1 item.

Logan Wright: Suggest v1: cost attribution boundaries = pool-level. If hosted uses dedicated temporarily, it’s still counted in dedicated pool spend (since that’s how billing hits). Add an “exceptions/limitations” note. Otherwise we’re inventing allocation we can’t validate.

Laura Bennett: I’m okay with that for v1 if we document it clearly.

Nadia Rahman: What about idle allocation basis within a pool? Options:
A) allocate idle $ across all dims proportionally to active GPU time
B) allocate idle $ across dims proportionally to output tokens
C) keep idle as separate and blended metric uses (active+idle)/tokens anyway
A and B are basically equivalent once you pick the denominator, but it affects drilldowns.

Mei Lin: If we want drilldowns by model/version/quant profile, token-weighted makes the most sense because that’s what the denominator is. Otherwise a model with long sequences could get misallocated if it uses more GPU per token.

Logan Wright: But that’s the point — GPU per token differences are what we’re trying to measure. If you allocate idle by tokens, you’re penalizing high volume models more even if they’re efficient.

Rishi Malhotra: Finance view: allocation should reflect what “consumes” the capacity. In our reporting, we allocate shared costs by usage volume (tokens/requests). It’s not perfect but consistent.

Jada Williams: Alternative: allocate idle by “reserved target capacity” per model route? But we don’t have stable mapping of reserved headroom per model today.

Sean Gallagher: Also headroom is a policy knob and changes with incident posture. We don’t want the KPI changing because SRE bumped headroom during a bad week.

Caleb Johnson: We can add a second metric: “active $/1M output tokens” where numerator excludes idle (only billable GPU-seconds while executing). That helps engineering diagnose efficiency while keeping blended for finance.

Laura Bennett: I like having both, but the dashboard needs one primary headline. “Blended (fully loaded)” as primary, “active-only” as diagnostic secondary.

Logan Wright: If we do both, we must ensure they share denominator and differ only in idle inclusion. Otherwise it becomes a mess.

Jada Williams: Summarizing what I’m hearing (tell me if wrong):

3) Allocation across dimensions should follow the same basis as denominator (output tokens), at least for v1.

Logan Wright: I’m not fully sold on token-weighted allocation for idle, but I can live with it if we keep pool boundaries strict and make sure the ADR states the tradeoff.

Cole Summers: I’ll update ADR-034 draft with:

- Overhead included in blended, excluded in active-only
- Pool-level attribution as first-class (hosted shared / dedicated / private)
- Dimension allocation basis = output tokens within pool

Caleb Johnson: From data quality: I’ll add a check that compares total output tokens (metering) vs expected tokens derived from request logs for streaming paths, since that’s where we’ve seen undercount. If mismatch > threshold, we mark blended metric as “low confidence.”

title: #architecture: 1737324412
preview:
Allison Grant: dropping the proposed audit event taxonomy naming conventions here for quick review. main goals: consistent, SIEM-friendly, low-cardinality. pls sanity check + call out footguns.

Allison Grant: current proposal (rough):
- `event_type`: lower_snake with dot segments: `<domain>.<entity>.<action>`
- action is verb in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/export.configure/etc)
- no IDs in `event_type` ever
- avoid logging raw diffs; include controlled summaries

Jordan Lee: +1 on dot segments. just want to make sure we don’t end up with 12 variants of “update”. can we define a small verb set and stick to it?

Allison Grant: yep, verb set is intended to be enumerated. draft list is like: create|update|delete|enable|disable|rotate|revoke|login|logout|impersonate|configure|export|install|upgrade|rollback

Kevin Osei: from SIEM side, dot segments are fine. Splunk folks usually build `event_type="rbac.role.update"` queries. pls don’t use camelCase.

Logan Wright: main concern: people will want to stuff “details” into event_type (e.g. `policy.fallback.update.added_region_us-east-1`). should be hard no.

Allison Grant: hard no :slightly_smiling_face: details go into structured fields.

Ethan Park: question: do we want `rbac.role.update` or `rbac.role.updated`? i prefer present tense since it matches “action” field semantics.

Jordan Lee: present tense reads better in filters IMO. it’s an event record but the type is “what happened”. either way, pick one.

Marcus Lin: choose one and enforce. don’t bike shed for weeks :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: ok, present tense it is.

Nadia Rahman: also pls avoid `event_type` prefixes like `audit.` or `redwood.`. redundant and wastes bytes. if we need namespace, do it in `producer.service`.

Allison Grant: agreed. taxonomies should be deployment-agnostic. we have `deployment_mode` / `environment` fields separately.

Paula Mendes: can we talk cardinality? biggest offenders we’ve seen:
- `request_path`
- raw `user_agent`
- “diff” field with full JSON
- free-form `reason` / “note”

Logan Wright: +1. for coverage dashboards, if people put request_path we get insane dimension explosion.

Kevin Osei: on `user_agent`: some customers like it for investigations, but raw UA is too high cardinality. recommend normalized fields?

Allison Grant: proposed:
- keep `source.user_agent` optional but *normalized* (e.g., `"Chrome"`, `"curl"`, `"redwood-sdk"`), not the full string
- keep raw UA only in debug logs, not audit logs

Ethan Park: what about `source.ip`? that’s technically high cardinality but also necessary.

Allison Grant: `source.ip` is allowed (investigation-critical). we’ll keep it but treat it as “approved high-cardinality” and not index it in every internal metric.

Logan Wright: +1. in telemetry we can hash it or keep it out of labels. audit log itself can contain it.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes, people will want to include full before/after. can we define `change_summary` as a bounded structure?

Allison Grant: yep. suggestion:
- `change.action`: `"set"|"add"|"remove"` (enum)
- `change.fields`: allow-list of field names that changed (strings from enum)
- `change.before` / `change.after`: strings, capped length, must not contain secrets
- optional `change.diff_hash`: sha256 of canonicalized JSON if you really need integrity

Kevin Osei: love `diff_hash`. helps when exporter retries cause dupes and customer wants de-dupe / correlation.

Marcus Lin: careful: `before/after` values can leak PII if it’s an email, etc. make sure we have a classification table + redaction guidance.

Allison Grant: totally. we’re mapping every field to classification. and `before/after` is only for config-ish enums, not for user-provided text.

Nadia Rahman: concrete example might help service teams.

Allison Grant: example draft:
```
event_type=rbac.role.update
actor.type=user
actor.id=usr_...
actor.email_hash=sha256(...)
resource.type=rbac_role
resource.id=role_...
change.fields=["permissions","name"]
change.before="permissions:viewer"
change.after="permissions:admin"
```

Ethan Park: nit: `resource.type=rbac_role` vs `rbac.role`? want consistent shape.

Jordan Lee: i’d vote `resource.type` as enum-ish tokens like `rbac_role`, `api_key`, `routing_policy`. then `event_type` stays dotted.

Allison Grant: yep, that’s where we landed in the schema draft. `resource.type` is an enum registry.

Logan Wright: pls also ban `resource.name` unless it’s constrained. names are often user-provided.

Allison Grant: we can allow `resource.display_name` only if it’s a system-generated slug and capped (e.g. max 64 chars) + character allow-list.

Kevin Osei: for exports, customers sometimes want “project name”. if we can’t include it, ok, but at least include stable IDs so they can join on their side.

Allison Grant: yep: IDs always, names optional w/ constraints.

Paula Mendes: also: please don’t log full prompt text / model output in audit logs (i know it sounds obvious but we’ve seen it in the wild).

Marcus Lin: +1000. audit logs are not app logs.

Jordan Lee: for routing policy events, can we standardize naming like:
- `policy.routing.update`
- `policy.fallback.update`
- `policy.allowlist.update`
vs going deeper like `routing.policy.update`

Allison Grant: preference is domain-first by ownership surface:
- `routing_policy.update`
- `routing_policy.fallback.update`
BUT i’m worried that mixing underscores + dots might be confusing.

Logan Wright: keep it consistent: dots separate “levels”, underscores inside tokens. so `policy_engine.routing_policy.update` is too long. maybe `policy.routing.update` with resource.type to disambiguate.

Kevin Osei: from SIEM, shorter `event_type` is better. `policy.routing.update` is fine if resource.type says `routing_policy`.

Ethan Park: +1. event_type shouldn’t encode service internals. `policy.routing.update` is clear.

Allison Grant: ok, proposal update:
- use high-level domains: `rbac.*`, `credential.*`, `policy.*`, `export.*`, `retention.*`, `private_install.*`
- keep resource.type as the specific thing (`api_key`, `routing_policy`, `siem_destination`, `retention_policy`)

Jordan Lee: works. for private installer, do we really want `private_install.install`? that reads weird.

Allison Grant: maybe `private.install` / `private.upgrade` / `private.rollback`? consistent with domain = private.

Marcus Lin: ok with `private.*` as long as it’s explicitly for the Private product/installer actions.

Nadia Rahman: last request: can we document “do not include request_path” in schema examples + lints? people will do it by habit.

Allison Grant: yes. we’ll add lint rules:
- reject `request_path`
- reject any `*.raw` fields in audit events
- enforce length caps on free-text fields
- enforce `event_type` registry (no ad hoc strings)

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: if we do that + a small approved list of high-card fields (source.ip, trace_id, request_id), dashboards will stay sane.

Kevin Osei: pls include guidance on `user_agent` normalization in exporter docs too (customers ask).

Allison Grant: noted. i’ll update the draft and link in this thread once pushed. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: thanks. let’s lock naming this week so instrumentation doesn’t churn.

title: #eng: 1737228441
preview:
Sahana Iyer: trying to lock down an integration test plan for split accuracy (route experiments) before we ask for any kind of load test. what's the best way to validate allocation % + determinism without spinning up a giant traffic generator?

Leila Farouk: +1. e2e tests today can prove “request gets variant header” but not “50/50 is actually 50/50”. we also want something that runs in CI and doesn’t flap.

Jordan Lee: we can get most of the confidence with simulation (no GPUs, no real traffic). assignment is deterministic hash -> bucket -> variant. so test the assigner as a pure function.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
1) generate N synthetic stickiness keys (uuid4 / random 128-bit) with a fixed seed.
2) run `assign(experiment_config, key)` for each key.
3) count variant hits, assert within tolerance for expected split.
4) repeat for a few configs (50/50, 90/10, 34/33/33, tiny buckets) and for “missing key” paths.

Jordan Lee: importantly: treat it as a statistical test w/ deterministic input. don’t rely on true randomness.

Jada Williams: another thing: for multi-variant, use chi-square instead of per-variant tolerance. but per-variant bounds are easier to understand.

Vikram Singh: this covers the bucketing lib, but we also need router integration: ensure we’re not accidentally re-hashing different keys depending on headers present.

Vikram Singh: can do a lightweight integration test against router/policy engine in-process:
- stand up policy evaluator w/ an experiment config
- feed request contexts with different combinations of stickiness inputs (customer_id, user_id header, session_id, fallback)
- ensure key precedence matches ADR and assigned variant matches the pure assigner for the resolved key

Logan Wright: + reminder: if folks try to use metrics to validate split accuracy, it’s noisy in staging due to sampling + attribution drop. for correctness tests, prefer the pure assignment + router debug endpoint output.

Caleb Johnson: +1. telemetry is great for prod monitoring, not for CI validation of distribution.

Leila Farouk: can we put this under “integration tests” even if it’s mostly simulation? i’m thinking we keep it close to ENG-9031 scope.

Jordan Lee: yep. we can call it “distribution simulation test” but run it in the same suite.

Jordan Lee: rough pseudocode:

```python
import random
from collections import Counter

SEED = 1337
N = 20000

configs = [
  ("50-50", {"A": 0.5, "B": 0.5}),
  ("90-10", {"A": 0.9, "B": 0.1}),
  ("3-way", {"A": 0.34, "B": 0.33, "C": 0.33}),
]

def gen_keys(n):
  r = random.Random(SEED)
  return [f"user-{r.getrandbits(128):032x}" for _ in range(n)]

keys = gen_keys(N)

for name, split in configs:
  counts = Counter(assign(split, k) for k in keys)
  for v, p in split.items():
    # 5-sigma bound + small epsilon
    mu = N * p
    sigma = (N * p * (1 - p)) ** 0.5
    assert abs(counts[v] - mu) <= max(5*sigma, 0.005*N)
```

Jordan Lee: yes. for missing key behavior we should test:

- it reports which key source it used (customer_id vs header vs session)
- it reports config version
- it returns variant_id
then compare that variant_id to the pure assigner output.

3) a tiny smoke e2e just to ensure headers propagate (variant header present) (already have)

Jordan Lee: good call. in ADR we said hashing uses stable `variant_id` ordering + cumulative split. so if variant IDs change, assignment changes. we should make that explicit in tests + docs. for tests, assert that reordering variants in config does NOT change mapping (we normalize by variant_id sort) but changing variant_id does.

Jordan Lee: +1. and we should pin the seed so any failure is reproducible locally.

Sahana Iyer: cool. i’ll write up the test plan in ENG-9031 and link this thread. thanks all.

Leila Farouk: :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: and if anyone wants to cross-check in staging later, use the experiment health dashboard, but don’t block CI on telemetry numbers.

title: #eng-platform: 1736879041
preview:
Jada Williams: quick review request: request-context plumbing for route experiments → tracing/metrics. want to sanity check we’re propagating `{experiment_id, variant_id, config_version}` consistently across router -> runtime -> otel spans -> metrics emitters. code pointers:
- `redwood/router/request_context.go` (new fields on `RequestContext`)
- `redwood/policy/experiment_evaluator.go` (assignment)
- `redwood/observability/otel/span_tags.go` (tagging)
PRs: `pr-28470` + `pr-28489`. anything we’re missing before we cut beta?

Jordan Lee: looked this morning. overall shape good. 2 things:
1) please make sure we set assignment *before* fallback policy eval (otherwise we’ll sometimes tag spans with the fallback model’s “effective” selection but no experiment fields). i think `pr-28457` changed order but i want to confirm `RequestContext` gets populated in the same block.
2) determinism: assignment should only depend on normalized route_id + stickiness key + experiment_id + config_version. don’t include model_id in hash input.

Elena Popov: +1. current flow in `route_selection.go` is:
```go
// (1) resolve route
// (2) evaluate experiment
// (3) choose variant -> set ctx.Experiment*
// (4) apply canary/fallback policies
```
so i think order is ok.

Logan Wright: on the telemetry side: we want to avoid “half attribution” where otel spans have experiment tags but our metrics don’t (or vice versa). what’s the canonical source for the experiment fields? `RequestContext` seems fine, just ensure both tracing + metrics read from it.

Caleb Johnson: question on naming. in `span_tags.go` i saw:
- `redwood.experiment_id`
- `redwood.experiment_variant_id`
- `redwood.route_id`
We also have older conventions using `routing.*` tags. any preference?

Allison Grant: please keep it aligned with existing OTel conventions doc: prefix internal fields with `redwood.` is fine. don’t introduce `experiment.*` without a namespace (we’ll collide w vendors). so:
- `redwood.experiment_id`
- `redwood.variant_id` (shorter)
- `redwood.experiment_config_version`
Also: route_id should be the normalized template (not raw path) to keep cardinality sane.

Vikram Singh: on the request context propagation: does it survive streaming retries? i remember we had a bug where we re-created ctx for retry path and dropped metadata.

Jada Williams: yeah that’s exactly what i’m worried about. current code sets fields on `RequestContext` and we pass it through `req.Context()` + our internal struct. but the streaming retry is in `streaming/dispatcher.go` and it does:
```go
ctx := context.Background()
ctx = withTrace(ctx, parent)
// TODO: carry RequestContext
```
so it will drop experiment tags. i can patch.

Rafael Mendes: please patch before beta expand. missing attribution on retries will blow up drop-rate alerts and confuse customers. we should also add a unit/integration test that simulates streaming retry and checks tags are present.

Jordan Lee: +1. and for safety: if `RequestContext` is missing on some code path, make sure we set `redwood.experiment_id` to nothing (don’t set “unknown”). “unknown” creates a new label value and screws aggregations.

Caleb Johnson: re: metrics emitters — where do we tag?
- per-request metrics (`inference.request.latency_ms`, `inference.request.error_count`) tagged by `{route_id, model_id, region, experiment_id, variant_id}`
- token metrics are too high volume. we’re not tagging per-token w experiment dims right?

Logan Wright: for GA we agreed minimal set:
- request-level metrics: include experiment dims (required)
- token-level metrics: only include experiment dims in sampled traces, NOT in high-cardinality metrics
this matches the cardinality guidance in the metrics taxonomy notes.

Allison Grant: yep. keep experiment dims off any “by_token” counters/histograms. if we ever add cost/token, it should be derived from request totals (prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) with the experiment dims at request level.

Elena Popov: how are we surfacing assignment to the customer / debugging? are we still returning headers?

Jada Williams: currently:
- response headers always include `x-redwood-experiment-id` + `x-redwood-variant-id` when an experiment applied
- request logs include `experiment_id` and `variant_id`
I’m on the fence about always-on headers vs debug-only.

Rafael Mendes: please keep headers for beta (support needs them), but make it clear they’re informational. also add redaction / allowlist if we think ids leak sensitive info (probably low risk but worth a quick security glance).

Caleb Johnson: one more gotcha: the OTel span tagging happens in `otel/middleware.go` at request start. but experiment assignment happens after routing policy eval (later). so unless we update span attributes mid-request, root span won’t have experiment tags.

Jada Williams: good catch. currently we set attrs on child span (`routing.evaluate`) not the root span. that means dashboards that filter on request root span might miss it.

Allison Grant: we should set on the root span once assignment is known. ok to call `span.SetAttributes(...)` later in the request. just avoid doing it in a tight loop.

Logan Wright: +1. our trace pipeline supports post-hoc attrs. please also set the fields on the log context (structured log fields) from `RequestContext` so even if trace sampling drops, logs still have it.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
- `RequestContext` owns the canonical assignment
- after experiment evaluation, call a helper `observability.SyncExperimentAttrs(ctx)` that:
  - sets attrs on current span (and root if available)
  - updates logger fields
  - updates request-metrics tag set
So we don’t have 3 ad-hoc callsites.

Jada Williams: i like that. action items i’ll take:
1) fix streaming retry path to carry RequestContext (and add integration test)
2) update root span attrs after assignment (not only child span)
3) rename attrs to `redwood.variant_id` and `redwood.experiment_config_version` per Allison’s note
4) ensure we never emit “unknown” label values

Vikram Singh: can you link the exact files once you push? i’ll do a quick review for any other ctx recreation paths (webhooks + async eval hook have similar patterns).

Jada Williams: will do. i’ll open follow-up PR today and tag @Vikram Singh @Caleb Johnson @Allison Grant.

Rafael Mendes: once merged, ping me and i’ll update the oncall notes + attribution drop dashboards to expect retry tagging to improve (so we don’t chase false alarms).

Logan Wright: also please add a small debug log when experiment applied:
`"experiment_assignment", {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, config_version}`
but only at `DEBUG`.

Jada Williams: ack. thanks all.
