title: #eng-platform: 1736538810
preview:
Jordan Lee: seeing weird allocation skew on route experiments when stickiness key is missing. In staging + 1 beta acct, variant B basically gets ~0-2% even though split is 50/50.

Jordan Lee: suspecting we're hashing `""` or a super low-entropy fallback in the evaluator. can someone from telemetry sanity check whether `stickiness_key_present=false` correlates?

Logan Wright: yep. quick query on staging rollup: when `sticky_key_source=missing` we see almost all requests land in the same bucket.

Logan Wright: pasted from my scratchpad:
```
variant distribution (missing key):
A: 98.7%
B: 1.3%

variant distribution (customer_id):
A: 50.4%
B: 49.6%
```

Ethan Park: :eyes: do we know what the evaluator uses today when missing? last I remember we had precedence order: `x-redwood-sticky-key` -> `end_user_id` -> `customer_id` -> else ???

Jordan Lee: current code path does:
- if none found, `sticky = ""`
- hash("" + experiment_id + salt)

Jordan Lee: so yeah, every request hashes to same value => everyone goes to same variant.

Allison Grant: oof. that also explains why attribution drop didn’t show up; it’s not dropping, it’s just collapsing.

Mina Haddad: we should not silently do that. can we force a deterministic but not-constant fallback?

Jada Williams: product-wise we talked about “safe defaults” but this is *not* safe. if customer forgets to pass key, they think they’re running 50/50 but actually 99/1.

Dylan Brooks: +1, and support will get wrecked. we need Console to warn/block (at least warn loudly) when they configure an experiment without a viable stickiness source.

Benji Okafor: what are options?
1) hard fail experiment start if missing stickiness config / no eligible stable id
2) allow start but default to request_id (non-sticky) so split is correct but users can bounce variants
3) allow start with warning + fixed variant?? (pls no)

Jordan Lee: I’m leaning (2) in router/evaluator: if no stable id, use `request_id` (or trace_id) as entropy so distribution is correct. mark assignment as `non_sticky=true`.

Allison Grant: if we use request_id, at least p99/cost guardrails reflect real distribution. downside is per-user experience (flip-flopping). but that’s better than lying.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE perspective, also want a metric so we can alert if `sticky_key_missing_rate` spikes. otherwise we’ll keep rediscovering this.

Logan Wright: we can emit a counter or tag on span: `experiment.sticky_key_source` with limited cardinality (customer_id/user_id/header/missing). already aligned with ADR-048 naming.

Ethan Park: one more nuance: determinism across retries/streaming. if we use request_id, retries might generate new IDs and re-bucket mid-flight.

Jordan Lee: good call. we can use the gateway request id that’s stable across retries (we already have `x-redwood-request-id`). for streaming retries, I think it’s preserved (but we had that bug last week).

Logan Wright: in the streaming retry bug, the request context was being rebuilt and dropped experiment fields. but request-id header was preserved. so fallback entropy could still be consistent.

- warning callout if we can’t guarantee stable stickiness
- tooltip linking to SDK docs for `x-redwood-sticky-key`

Rafael Mendes: careful w false positives on small traffic. maybe gate that on min sample size.

Ethan Park: agree. start with missing-key banner + logging; skew detection can be follow-up.

variant distribution (missing key):

B: 1.3%

variant distribution (customer_id):
A: 50.4%

- hash("" + experiment_id + salt)

title: #eng-ml: 1736372044
preview:
Marissa Cole: hey folks — need to lock down what “quality” means in Console for route experiments. We keep saying “quality metric” but it’s fuzzy. For beta we need something we can defend + explain in UI. Can we align today on: (1) what eval score(s) we surface, (2) normalization (so it’s not random 0.73 vs 0.81), (3) drift / when to invalidate.

Jonas Weber: +1. From eval harness POV we can emit a few different things:
- per-prompt scores (raw)
- per-suite aggregate (weighted avg)
- task breakdown (format adherence, correctness proxy, safety, etc)
Question is what becomes the single number in Console.

Selene Huang: also pls define if the number is absolute or “delta vs control”. If it’s absolute, customers will assume it’s comparable across routes/models which it won’t be.

Olivia Grant: for integration we already have a planned stream like:
`{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, eval_suite_id, score_name, raw_score, ts}`
We can also add `normalized_score` but need to know how we normalize.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: constraints from Applied ML:
- Quality must be *interpret-able* and stable across time.
- Avoid “one metric to rule them all” that gets gamed.
- For initial release, I’d rather show a *small set* of canonical scores than a custom free-for-all.

Alicia Perez: UI question: are we thinking a single “Quality” card (green/red) + optional breakdown drawer? We can do tooltips + “based on Redwood eval suite” copy, but we need crisp semantics.

Logan Wright: telemetry note: whatever we pick, please avoid exploding cardinality (score_name x suite x route x variant x region). We can handle a couple of fields, but not a dozen per request. If it’s asynchronous evals (not per request), easier.

Marissa Cole: yep — this is *not* per-request. It’s optional eval pipeline attribution per variant, and Console shows it alongside latency/cost/error. But we need a default “quality” definition.

Jonas Weber: proposal for beta:
- Default eval suite: `route_experiments_v1`
- Suite produces 0..1 for each prompt with a rubric; aggregate is weighted mean
- Console shows:
  1) “Quality (eval)” = aggregate suite score (0..100)
  2) “Δ vs control” = variant_score - control_score (in points)
This keeps absolute + delta.

Selene Huang: I’m strongly against showing the absolute as 0..100 without a big caveat. People will compare across routes, then complain it’s inconsistent.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. Absolute is fine only if it’s clearly “within this prompt set + eval suite” and not an overall product KPI.

Alicia Perez: we can label it “Eval score (route prompt set)” and default the main UI to delta. Absolute could be in the breakdown panel.

Marissa Cole: delta-first makes sense. But customers also ask “is variant B better” and a delta needs context if control is 12 vs 92.

Jonas Weber: we can show both but make delta prominent.

Logan Wright: if we show both, we need to ensure both are computed on the same sampling window + filters. Otherwise support tickets.

Olivia Grant: re: normalization — raw scores from different rubrics aren’t comparable. Even within a suite, scores can drift as we update prompts or judge model. We need a stable mapping.

Selene Huang: on drift: prompt set changes should invalidate history. Also if we update the judge model / rubric version, we should reset or version the metric.

Jonas Weber: +1. We can bake versioning into eval suite id, e.g. `route_experiments_v1` and bump to v2 when rubric/judge changes.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: normalization suggestion (simple, defensible):
- For each eval suite version + prompt set id, compute score distribution for the *control* variant during first N hours/days.
- Report variant deltas in “points” and optionally z-score relative to control distribution.
- If control distribution drifts beyond threshold (e.g., prompt set edits), flag “baseline changed” and stop showing trend.
No need for fancy stat-sig.

Marissa Cole: do we have “prompt set id” today as a real concept in product, or is it internal?

Jonas Weber: internal today, but we can surface it as “Eval configuration” in the UI (read-only). For beta, it may just be “Redwood default prompt set” vs “Customer-provided prompt set” (if we allow attach).

Olivia Grant: for Phase 2 we planned “attach eval suite” in Console. I’d rather treat prompt set as explicit object with id + version.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in — from Console standpoint, we can show:
- Quality: “Not configured” state unless user attaches evals
- If configured: show delta prominently; absolute available in tooltip
But PM wants defaults to “just work” for beta design partners.

Marissa Cole: yes, beta ask is “I swap model variants; tell me if outputs got worse.” So default eval suite + prompt set must exist.

Selene Huang: default prompt set can’t be generic across customers. It needs to match the route use case or it’s misleading.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: there’s a middle ground: default “sanity suite” that checks generic regressions:
- refusal / policy compliance (don’t go off the rails)
- formatting / JSON validity (if structured output route)
- extreme toxicity
But that’s not “quality” in the helpfulness sense.

Jonas Weber: we can scope default to: “format adherence + safety + hallucination proxy” and call it “Output quality (sanity checks)”. That’s closer to what we can defend broadly.

Alicia Perez: naming matters a lot. If we call it “Quality” people assume it means “better answers.” If it’s safety/format, it should be labeled.

Marissa Cole: ok, so two options:
A) Keep label “Quality” but define it as “Eval score for your attached prompt set” (requires configuration)
B) Provide default “Sanity score” always on (route-agnostic) and call it that
We can do both? (sanity always, quality optional)

Logan Wright: two metrics is fine if they’re async aggregates. Please keep to a small set of score_names: `sanity_score`, `task_score` (optional) and maybe `format_score` if needed.

Olivia Grant: +1. In eval output we can emit:
- `sanity_score` (0..1)
- `quality_score` (0..1) only when prompt set attached
- `n_samples`
- `suite_version`
That’s manageable.

Selene Huang: and we should *never* average scores across different prompt sets. If prompt set changes, show “insufficient data” until we have enough samples again.

Jonas Weber: sampling: we need to avoid bias when split is small. I propose:
- nightly job per experiment
- target 200 prompts per variant per run (cap)
- minimum 30 prompts before showing anything
- if a variant has low traffic, we sample from recent traffic logs with deterministic seed to avoid flapping

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: careful: sampling from traffic logs can introduce privacy / PII issues depending on customer data. For now, do we have a safe “prompt capture” pipeline? Otherwise we should require customer-provided prompt set for “quality_score”.

Marissa Cole: good point. For beta we *do* have 2 design partners willing to provide prompt sets. Let’s not promise auto-capture in GA.

Dylan Brooks: that fits the PRD: “optional integration to attach eval scores (prompt set quality signals)”. So default “quality_score” only exists if attached.

Alicia Perez: UI plan then:
- Card 1: “Sanity (eval)” always available (if we can run generic checks without customer prompts??)
- Card 2: “Quality (eval)” only when eval config attached
But if we can’t run sanity without prompts, both become “attach prompt set” anyway.

Jonas Weber: sanity still needs prompts. Unless it’s run on synthetic prompts, which isn’t representative. I’d rather not show it unless attached.

Selene Huang: +1. Synthetic prompts = false confidence.

Marissa Cole: ok: in beta + GA, “quality” metrics appear only if user attaches eval configuration (prompt set + suite). Otherwise Console shows “No quality data configured” with CTA.

Olivia Grant: then normalization: can we agree on:
- store raw 0..1
- display as 0..100 “points”
- main display is Δ points vs control
- also show absolute points in tooltip

Selene Huang: yes, but require we also show `n_samples` and a “data freshness” timestamp. Otherwise we get “quality not updating” tickets.

Logan Wright: +100. Also we should emit `eval_run_id` so support can correlate.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: drift rules / invalidation:
- prompt set has version; if version changes, reset the time series (new baseline)
- eval suite version changes => reset baseline
- if control changes model_id mid-experiment (shouldn’t, but can happen), flag and stop computing deltas until stable

Jonas Weber: we can enforce “control variant immutable while experiment running” at config validation layer. If model changes, require stop + new experiment.

Marissa Cole: that’s aligned with rollouts constraints, but there are cases where people update a pinned model version. We should at least detect and banner.

Dylan Brooks: banner copy idea: “Quality comparisons paused: control variant changed (model/version). Create a new experiment to resume.”

Alicia Perez: works.

Selene Huang: what about multi-metric suites? e.g. helpfulness + correctness + style. Do we collapse into one number?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: for now, yes, one “quality_score” per suite. But include breakdown in JSON (internal) for future UI.

Olivia Grant: we can include breakdown as extra fields in the eval artifact, but Console API can initially only expose aggregate to avoid more UI work.

Logan Wright: + keep the storage flexible but the surfaced metrics minimal.

Marissa Cole: summarizing what I’m hearing (tell me if wrong):
1) “Quality” in Console = eval suite aggregate score attached to experiment variants; not computed unless user attaches eval config.
2) Display delta vs control as primary; absolute score secondary.
3) Score shown as 0..100 points derived from raw 0..1.
4) Versioning: prompt set version + eval suite version are part of identity; if either changes, reset baseline and show “insufficient data”.
5) Show sample size + last run timestamp; if control changes, pause comparisons + banner.

Jonas Weber: yes. Also we should define default suite when attaching: `route_experiments_quality_v1` (name bikeshed) with documented rubric.

Selene Huang: +1, and we should avoid the term “statistically significant” anywhere. Just “directional” + guardrails.

Alicia Perez: UX action item for me: update label from “Quality” to “Eval score” in the first iteration, with tooltip explaining it’s specific to the attached prompt set + suite. If PM insists on “Quality”, we need a stronger tooltip.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with “Eval score” as the label. “Quality” can be the section header maybe.

Olivia Grant: engineering action item: I’ll update the integration doc to include these required fields:
- `eval_suite_id`, `eval_suite_version`
- `prompt_set_id`, `prompt_set_version`
- `score_name=quality_score`
- `raw_score`, `score_points`
- `n_samples`, `eval_run_ts`
And add “reset baseline on version change” behavior.

Logan Wright: I’ll sync w/ Caleb/Mei on the dataset schema so we don’t break rollups. Also will confirm retention — eval runs are lower volume but we should still cap.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: I’ll draft a short definition for docs: “Eval score measures performance on your attached prompt set using Redwood’s evaluation suite. It’s best-effort directional signal, not a guarantee.”

Marissa Cole: perfect. I’ll paste this into PRD + ping Console folks. thanks all :thumbsup:

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: pls also add a note: “Do not compare scores across different routes or prompt sets.”

Alicia Perez: adding to tooltip + empty state.

Jonas Weber: I’ll also add a “minimum samples” recommendation (>=30) to avoid noisy deltas. Might be a hard threshold in API.

Dylan Brooks: if we hard threshold, make sure the results API returns `insufficient_data=true` so UI can handle gracefully.

Olivia Grant: yep, will include. end.

Selene Huang: default prompt set can’t be generic across customers. It needs to match the route use case or it’s misleading.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: there’s a middle ground: default “sanity suite” that checks generic regressions:
- refusal / policy compliance (don’t go off the rails)
- formatting / JSON validity (if structured output route)
- extreme toxicity
But that’s not “quality” in the helpfulness sense.

Jonas Weber: we can scope default to: “format adherence + safety + hallucination proxy” and call it “Output quality (sanity checks)”. That’s closer to what we can defend broadly.

Alicia Perez: naming matters a lot. If we call it “Quality” people assume it means “better answers.” If it’s safety/format, it should be labeled.

Marissa Cole: ok, so two options:
A) Keep label “Quality” but define it as “Eval score for your attached prompt set” (requires configuration)
B) Provide default “Sanity score” always on (route-agnostic) and call it that
We can do both? (sanity always, quality optional)

Logan Wright: two metrics is fine if they’re async aggregates. Please keep to a small set of score_names: `sanity_score`, `task_score` (optional) and maybe `format_score` if needed.

Olivia Grant: +1. In eval output we can emit:
- `sanity_score` (0..1)
- `quality_score` (0..1) only when prompt set attached
- `n_samples`
- `suite_version`
That’s manageable.

Selene Huang: and we should *never* average scores across different prompt sets. If prompt set changes, show “insufficient data” until we have enough samples again.

Jonas Weber: sampling: we need to avoid bias when split is small. I propose:
- nightly job per experiment
- target 200 prompts per variant per run (cap)
- minimum 30 prompts before showing anything
- if a variant has low traffic, we sample from recent traffic logs with deterministic seed to avoid flapping

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: careful: sampling from traffic logs can introduce privacy / PII issues depending on customer data. For now, do we have a safe “prompt capture” pipeline? Otherwise we should require customer-provided prompt set for “quality_score”.

Marissa Cole: good point. For beta we *do* have 2 design partners willing to provide prompt sets. Let’s not promise auto-capture in GA.

Dylan Brooks: that fits the PRD: “optional integration to attach eval scores (prompt set quality signals)”. So default “quality_score” only exists if attached.

Alicia Perez: UI plan then:
- Card 1: “Sanity (eval)” always available (if we can run generic checks without customer prompts??)
- Card 2: “Quality (eval)” only when eval config attached
But if we can’t run sanity without prompts, both become “attach prompt set” anyway.

Jonas Weber: sanity still needs prompts. Unless it’s run on synthetic prompts, which isn’t representative. I’d rather not show it unless attached.

Selene Huang: +1. Synthetic prompts = false confidence.

Marissa Cole: ok: in beta + GA, “quality” metrics appear only if user attaches eval configuration (prompt set + suite). Otherwise Console shows “No quality data configured” with CTA.

Olivia Grant: then normalization: can we agree on:
- store raw 0..1
- display as 0..100 “points”
- main display is Δ points vs control
- also show absolute points in tooltip

Selene Huang: yes, but require we also show `n_samples` and a “data freshness” timestamp. Otherwise we get “quality not updating” tickets.

Logan Wright: +100. Also we should emit `eval_run_id` so support can correlate.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: drift rules / invalidation:
- prompt set has version; if version changes, reset the time series (new baseline)
- eval suite version changes => reset baseline
- if control changes model_id mid-experiment (shouldn’t, but can happen), flag and stop computing deltas until stable

Jonas Weber: we can enforce “control variant immutable while experiment running” at config validation layer. If model changes, require stop + new experiment.

Marissa Cole: that’s aligned with rollouts constraints, but there are cases where people update a pinned model version. We should at least detect and banner.

Dylan Brooks: banner copy idea: “Quality comparisons paused: control variant changed (model/version). Create a new experiment to resume.”

Alicia Perez: works.

Selene Huang: what about multi-metric suites? e.g. helpfulness + correctness + style. Do we collapse into one number?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: for now, yes, one “quality_score” per suite. But include breakdown in JSON (internal) for future UI.

Olivia Grant: we can include breakdown as extra fields in the eval artifact, but Console API can initially only expose aggregate to avoid more UI work.

Logan Wright: + keep the storage flexible but the surfaced metrics minimal.

Marissa Cole: summarizing what I’m hearing (tell me if wrong):
1) “Quality” in Console = eval suite aggregate score attached to experiment variants; not computed unless user attaches eval config.
2) Display delta vs control as primary; absolute score secondary.
3) Score shown as 0..100 points derived from raw 0..1.
4) Versioning: prompt set version + eval suite version are part of identity; if either changes, reset baseline and show “insufficient data”.
5) Show sample size + last run timestamp; if control changes, pause comparisons + banner.

Jonas Weber: yes. Also we should define default suite when attaching: `route_experiments_quality_v1` (name bikeshed) with documented rubric.

Selene Huang: +1, and we should avoid the term “statistically significant” anywhere. Just “directional” + guardrails.

Alicia Perez: UX action item for me: update label from “Quality” to “Eval score” in the first iteration, with tooltip explaining it’s specific to the attached prompt set + suite. If PM insists on “Quality”, we need a stronger tooltip.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with “Eval score” as the label. “Quality” can be the section header maybe.

Olivia Grant: engineering action item: I’ll update the integration doc to include these required fields:
- `eval_suite_id`, `eval_suite_version`
- `prompt_set_id`, `prompt_set_version`
- `score_name=quality_score`
- `raw_score`, `score_points`
- `n_samples`, `eval_run_ts`
And add “reset baseline on version change” behavior.

Logan Wright: I’ll sync w/ Caleb/Mei on the dataset schema so we don’t break rollups. Also will confirm retention — eval runs are lower volume but we should still cap.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: I’ll draft a short definition for docs: “Eval score measures performance on your attached prompt set using Redwood’s evaluation suite. It’s best-effort directional signal, not a guarantee.”

Marissa Cole: perfect. I’ll paste this into PRD + ping Console folks. thanks all :thumbsup:

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: pls also add a note: “Do not compare scores across different routes or prompt sets.”

Alicia Perez: adding to tooltip + empty state.

Jonas Weber: I’ll also add a “minimum samples” recommendation (>=30) to avoid noisy deltas. Might be a hard threshold in API.

Dylan Brooks: if we hard threshold, make sure the results API returns `insufficient_data=true` so UI can handle gracefully.

Olivia Grant: yep, will include. end.

title: #eng-platform: 1736039442
preview:
Ethan Park: seeing weird config propagation lag in staging for route experiments. console says experiment started but router still serving control for ~6-10 mins. anyone else noticing?

Benji Okafor: yeah, hit it twice today. `experiment-service` shows state=RUNNING + config_version incremented, but policy engine evaluator still pulling old snapshot.

Jordan Lee: +1. I was debugging split skew and noticed the evaluator using `config_version=118` while control plane already at `124`.

Mina Haddad: is this only staging? prod seems ok (but lower churn).

Rafael Mendes: if lag is real we should get ahead of it before beta expansion. do we have a metric for “config staleness” at router/policy layer?

Michael Nguyen: we have `config_cache_hit` and `config_refresh_ms` but nothing directly comparing expected version vs observed version.

Ethan Park: dropping some logs. from experiment-service (staging):
```
2025-01-03T18:07:11.903Z INFO experiment.update actor=marissa_cole exp_id=exp_7f3c route_id=rt_204 action=START from=DRAFT to=RUNNING config_version=124
2025-01-03T18:07:12.014Z INFO publish.config_update topic=experiment-config-updates key=rt_204 version=124
```
policy-engine (staging) ~8 mins later:
```
2025-01-03T18:15:41.227Z DEBUG evaluator.load route=rt_204 cache=HIT version=118 age_s=523
2025-01-03T18:15:41.229Z DEBUG evaluator.decision route=rt_204 exp=exp_7f3c variant=control reason="no_active_experiment_in_snapshot"
```

Caleb Johnson: quick q: evaluator cache hit suggests it never refreshed. do we rely on pubsub invalidation in staging or periodic poll?

Jordan Lee: both. default is pubsub invalidation + periodic 60s poll as backstop. but that log shows `cache=HIT` and age_s > 500 which shouldn’t happen if poll is working.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side: I’m seeing “results not updating” complaints because attribution dims do show exp_id/variant_id only after router starts applying it. dashboard looks flat until then.

Sean Gallagher: do we know if this is “config not delivered” vs “delivered but not applied”? i.e. could be router not reloading policy engine bundle.

Vikram Singh: router pulls policy snapshot from policy-engine via internal gRPC. if evaluator hasn’t refreshed, router won’t see it. so likely upstream.

Amara Diallo: any chance staging pubsub is dropping messages? we had flaky nats in staging last week.

Michael Nguyen: we should check subscriber lag + drop counters on `experiment-config-updates` in staging.

Allison Grant: +1. also want to make sure we aren’t tagging metrics with raw route path creating cardinality issues, but separate topic.

Ethan Park: hypothesis list so far:
1) pubsub subscriber not connected / lagging
2) evaluator poller thread stuck / dead
3) cache TTL misconfigured in staging (too long) OR timestamp bug
4) config update published but filtered due to route_id normalization mismatch (rt_204 vs /v1/chat/...)

Jordan Lee: #4 is real possibility. we recently changed route-id normalization rules (template vs raw) in staging? i saw a patch in routing framework.

Benji Okafor: experiment-service publishes by `route_id` (internal id), so mismatch would have to be in evaluator subscription keying.

Caleb Johnson: can someone run a quick “explain” on staging using the debug endpoint? if it prints config version + last refresh time.

Jordan Lee: did that. output:
```
GET /internal/experiments/debug/route/rt_204
{
  "route_id": "rt_204",
  "snapshot_version": 118,
  "snapshot_loaded_at": "2025-01-03T18:06:58Z",
  "last_invalidation_event": null,
  "poller_last_run": "2025-01-03T18:07:00Z",
  "poller_status": "stopped" 
}
```
Poller stopped :grimacing:

Sean Gallagher: ok that’s the smoking gun. why is poller stopped? panic? thread crash? OOM?

Jordan Lee: checking earlier logs:
```
2025-01-03T18:07:00.112Z ERROR config.poller failed err="context deadline exceeded" target="experiment-service:443" attempt=3
2025-01-03T18:07:00.112Z ERROR config.poller entering_backoff backoff_s=600
```
so it’s not “stopped”, it’s in 10 min backoff. debug endpoint labels it poorly.

Rafael Mendes: 10 min backoff on staging explains the 6-10 min lag. but why are we timing out to experiment-service? that’s in same cluster.

Michael Nguyen: is experiment-service behind auth in staging? could be token refresh failing causing retries.

Benji Okafor: experiment-service p99 for `GetActiveConfig` in staging spiked earlier.
```
2025-01-03T18:06:59Z p99=3.8s p95=1.9s errors=0
```
client timeout on policy side might be 2s.

Ethan Park: yeah, policy-engine uses 2s deadline (copied from other config sources). staging DB was under load due to migration tests.

Sean Gallagher: action: reduce blast radius by not backing off for 10 mins on a single timeout. we need: bounded staleness and faster recovery.

Allison Grant: +1. and metric/alerting: “config propagation lag” measured end-to-end.

Logan Wright: metrics proposal (minimal):
- `experiment_config_snapshot_age_seconds{service=policy-engine}` (now - snapshot_loaded_at)
- `experiment_config_fetch_latency_ms{service=policy-engine}`
- `experiment_config_fetch_errors_total{code}`
- `experiment_config_version{service=experiment-service}` as gauge? maybe too expensive.

Caleb Johnson: gauge for version is tricky; better is compute “lag” via embedding `config_version` in event + in evaluator snapshot; emit a delta:
- `experiment_config_version_lag{route_id?}` but route_id tagging could explode.

Allison Grant: do NOT tag by route_id in metrics. maybe sample top routes or emit only aggregate percentiles. we can do logs/traces for per-route.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. for alerts we want global + maybe per-region.
Suggested alerts:
- snapshot_age p95 > 120s for 10m (warning)
- snapshot_age p95 > 300s for 5m (page?)
- invalidation drop rate > X%

Jordan Lee: also propose a dedicated metric on router side: when request is evaluated, include `policy_snapshot_age_s` as span attribute. can build histogram from traces.

Caleb Johnson: span attr is fine; but for metrics pipeline we should have an explicit histogram in policy-engine itself.

Ethan Park: near-term fix ideas:
1) decrease backoff cap from 600s -> 30s and keep polling
2) raise deadline from 2s -> 5s OR make it adaptive
3) add circuit breaker so we don’t DOS experiment-service when it’s slow

Benji Okafor: #1 feels safest for now. 10 min is too aggressive.

Sean Gallagher: caution: don’t remove backoff entirely; if downstream is hard down we’ll spin. but 10 min staleness is unacceptable for “start/stop” semantics.

Mina Haddad: can we also use pubsub invalidation more reliably? if event delivered, evaluator can refresh without waiting for poll.

Michael Nguyen: we still need poll as backstop (pubsub can drop). but we can change backoff logic: invalidation should immediately trigger refresh attempt even during backoff.

Jordan Lee: yes. current code uses a shared limiter; backoff blocks both poll + invalidation refresh. that’s likely the root design issue.

DeployBot: buildkite #staging-policy-engine build 8821 failed (unit tests). link: https://buildkite.redwood.example/policy-engine/builds/8821

Ethan Park: ok, who can take the code change? Jordan?

Jordan Lee: I can patch evaluator to separate backoff domains: fetch failures backoff only affects polling, invalidations still enqueue refresh with independent short jitter. plus we’ll rename debug endpoint fields to not say “stopped”.

Rafael Mendes: please also add the metrics we discussed. at minimum `snapshot_age_seconds` and `refresh_attempts_total{result}`.

Logan Wright: telemetry can help add a derived “propagation lag” metric computed as (now - config_version_created_at) if we carry `created_at` in config payload. do we have that?

Benji Okafor: config object has `updated_at` already. we can include it in evaluator snapshot and compute lag without needing version gauge.

Allison Grant: perfect. Metric definition:
- `experiment_config_propagation_lag_seconds` = now - snapshot.updated_at
No route_id labels. labels: env, region, service.

Sean Gallagher: add dashboard panels + alert rules draft. also add a runbook link once we have it.

Ethan Park: next steps:
- Jordan: PR for evaluator backoff + metrics
- Benji/Michael: check experiment-service staging latency + DB load; confirm if anything else causing timeouts
- Logan/Caleb/Allison: align on metric names + OTel tags (keep low cardinality)
- Rafael: propose alert thresholds; we’ll tune after a day of data

Jordan Lee: sounds good. I’ll open a PR and reference this thread.

Benji Okafor: I’ll look at experiment-service slow traces around 18:06-18:08.

Sean Gallagher: ping me when metric exists; I want it on the SRE “beta readiness” checklist.

Ethan Park: thanks all. will update when PR is up.
